era_context: The early 10th century witnessed the fragmentation of the Abbasid Caliphate and the rise of regional Islamic dynasties. In East Asia, the Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period was in its final phase before Song unification. In Europe, feudal consolidation continued under figures like Otto I. The Byzantine Empire remained a major Mediterranean power. This was a period of significant political reorganization across Eurasia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Americas (no written records for this period)

events.title: Later Tang Dynasty Collapse in Northern China
events.description: The Later Tang Dynasty, one of the Five Dynasties, collapsed in 935 as internal conflicts and military instability weakened central authority. The collapse of Later Tang marked the transition to the Later Jin Dynasty under Shi Jingtang. This event was part of the broader pattern of dynastic fragmentation and consolidation that characterized the Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period.
key_figures: Li Cunxu (Later Tang emperor, r. 923–926, deceased)

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)
Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms historical records

events.certainty_note: The Later Tang Dynasty's collapse is well-documented in Chinese historical sources and accepted by modern scholarship.

events.title: Goryeo Dynasty Established in Korea
events.description: The Goryeo Dynasty was established in Korea in 935, following the unification of the Korean peninsula under Wang Geon. The Goryeo Dynasty would rule Korea for nearly 500 years and would give its name to the modern country of Korea. The establishment of Goryeo marked the end of the Three Kingdoms period and the beginning of a unified Korean state.
key_figures: Wang Geon (Taejo of Goryeo, founder and first king, r. 918–943)

sources.name: Samguk Sagi (History of the Three Kingdoms)

events.certainty_note: The Goryeo Dynasty's establishment is well-documented in Korean historical sources. The date 935 is accepted by modern scholarship, though some sources give 918 as the founding date of the kingdom before formal establishment of the dynasty.

events.title: Henry the Fowler's Death and Otto I's Succession
events.description: Henry the Fowler, King of Germany and founder of the Saxon dynasty, died in 935. His death marked the end of an era and the beginning of his son Otto I's rise to power. Henry had consolidated German power and established the foundations for what would become the Holy Roman Empire. Otto I would build upon his father's legacy to create one of medieval Europe's most powerful states.
key_figures: Henry the Fowler (King of Germany, r. 919–936, died 935)

sources.name: Widukind of Corvey, Res gestae Saxonicae

events.certainty_note: Henry the Fowler's death in 935 is well-documented in contemporary chronicles. The date is accepted across modern scholarship.

events.title: Samanid Empire Flourishing in Central Asia
events.description: The Samanid Empire, centered in Bukhara and Samarkand, was at the height of its cultural and intellectual flourishing during the 10th century. By 935, the Samanids had established themselves as patrons of Persian literature, science, and philosophy. This period saw the development of Persian as a literary language and the flourishing of Islamic scholarship.
events.certainty_note: The Samanid Empire's cultural flourishing is well-documented in Islamic sources and is accepted by modern scholarship.

events.title: Abbasid Caliphate Fragmentation Continues
events.description: The Abbasid Caliphate, centered in Baghdad, continued its decline as regional dynasties asserted independence. By 935, the Caliph's authority was largely ceremonial, with real power held by regional governors and military strongmen. This fragmentation characterized the early 10th century Islamic world and created space for competing powers like the Fatimids and the Samanids.
events.certainty_note: The Abbasid Caliphate's fragmentation is well-documented in Islamic sources and is accepted by modern scholarship as a defining feature of this period.

disconfirming_evidence: The Goryeo Dynasty's founding date is sometimes given as 918 (when Wang Geon established his kingdom) rather than 935 (when he formally proclaimed the Goryeo Dynasty). Modern scholarship recognizes both dates as significant but emphasizes that the formal establishment of the dynasty occurred in 935. Henry the Fowler's death is sometimes presented as marking the end of an era, but modern scholarship recognizes that his policies and institutions continued to shape German politics under Otto I. The narrative of 'fragmentation' in the Islamic world can obscure the fact that regional dynasties like the Fatimids and Samanids represented sophisticated, stable governance.
historiographic_note: The year 935 is well-documented for East Asian and European events, with contemporary or near-contemporary sources available. Henry the Fowler's death is particularly well-attested in contemporary German chronicles. The Goryeo Dynasty's establishment is documented in later Korean sources, introducing some dating uncertainty. Islamic sources are abundant but reflect the perspectives of competing dynasties. Chinese sources on the Five Dynasties period are detailed but were compiled during the Song Dynasty, potentially introducing bias toward the eventual victors. African and Central Asian documentation remains sparse.

graph_edges.note: Henry the Fowler's death led to Otto I's coronation as King of Germany
Later Tang Dynasty's collapse led to Later Jin Dynasty's establishment
Goryeo Dynasty's establishment led to nearly 500 years of Korean history before Joseon Dynasty
